EVENT_NARRATIVE: A cooperative observer at Halfway reported ten inches of new snow and trained spotter near Sumpter reported eight inches. SNOTEL sites in the area recorded from ten to fifteen inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An intense winter storm brought high winds and heavy mountain snow to parts of Southwest Idaho and Southeast Oregon from January fourth through fifth. Wind gusts to ninety six miles per hour and six to eight inches of snow were reported in the mountains. Gusts to sixty four miles per hour including wind damage to trees and power lines were reported in the Treasure and Magic Valleys of Southwest Idaho.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rome, Oregon ASOS recorded a wind gust of sixty nine MPH and several RAWS stations reported winds over sixty MPH including a sustained wind of sixty three and a gust to ninety six MPH at Riddle Mountain RAWS.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flagstaff Hill RAWS station at an elevation of 3945 feet and Morgan Mountain RAWS station at 4200 feet recorded wind gusts to sixty MPH.

Heavy rainfall, fueled by a tropical moisture source that had origins in the west Pacific, drenched the region over the course of a few days. This heavy rainfall caused flooding along multiple rivers in northwest Oregon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trask River Road at milepost 7.5 was reported closed due to high water.  The was caused by the heavy rain that fell over the Coast Range, that resulted in the flooding of the Trask River.  A woman driving a car was stuck in the high water along the road, but was rescued.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Butte Falls woman died early Wednesday 11/12/2008 when her minivan veered off Butte Falls Highway and struck a tree. Jackson County deputies identified the woman as Juanita Mae Moore, 66, of Broad Street.||A passing driver spotted the single-vehicle crash in the 11000 block of the highway leading to and from Butte Falls at 7:39 a.m.||Moore's 2003 Pontiac Montana had left the road and hit a tree about 150 feet from the pavement.  The pavement was wet and a light fog blanketed the area of the crash.||The weather was probably a contributing factor, said Sheriff Mike Winters.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Lightning from a thunderstorm event on 08/16/08 ignited a round of wildfires across southwest Oregon. The Lonesome Complex fires consisted of the Middle Fork, Lonesome, and Bessie Rock fires. As of 09/01/08, all but the Middle Fork fires were contained. The Middle Fork fire was still burning when the last update was issued on 10/09/08.  At that time, the size of the fire was 21175 acres and the was 90% contained. The estimated containment date was 10/15/08.  No update on expenditures was available, but as of 09/01/08, 17.7 million dollars had been spent on firefighting efforts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning from a thunderstorm event on 08/16/08 ignited a round of wildfires across southwest Oregon. The estimated containment date was 10/15/08. No update on expenditures was available, but as of 09/01/08, 17.7 million dollars had been spent on firefighting efforts.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Lightning from a thunderstorm event on 08/17/08 ignited another round of wildfires across southwest Oregon. The Rattle Fire was initially part of the North Fork Complex, but the other fires were suppressed. The last update on this fire was issued on 10/07/08.  At that time, the size of the fire was 19775 acres and was 90% contained.  The estimated containment date was 10/30/08. No update on expenditures was available, but as of 08/31/08, 1.9 million dollars had been spent on firefighting efforts.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused moderate flooding on the Coquille River at Coquille as well as the North and South Forks of the Coquille River at Myrtle Point.  Although flooding was verified on these rivers, the only one of the three points that had a functioning and calibrated flood gage was the South Fork of the Coquille River at Myrtle Point.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A Flood Warning was issued for the North Fork of the Coquille River at Myrtle Point at 28/1926 PST, effective from the morning of 12/29 until the evening of 12/29.  It was extended until the afternoon of 12/30 at 29/2251 PST.  The river exceeded flood stage (35.0 feet)at 29/1315 PST, crested at 36.02 feet at 29/2030 PST, then dropped below flood stage at 30/0300 PST.  The flood warning was cancelled at 30/0409 PST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused moderate flooding on the Coquille River at Coquille as well as the North and South Forks of the Coquille River at Myrtle Point.  Although flooding was verified on these rivers, the only one of the three points that had a functioning and calibrated flood gage was the South Fork of the Coquille River at Myrtle Point.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A Flood Warning was issued for the Coquille River at Coquille at 28/1005 PST, effective from the afternoon of 12/28 until the morning of 12/30/08.  The warning was extended three times:  to the morning of 12/31 at 29/1806 PST, the afternoon of 12/30 at 30/0945 PST, and to the morning of the 12/31 at 30/1717 PST.  The river exceeded flood stage (21.0 feet) at 29/0800 PST, crested at 23.03 feet at 30/0300 PST, then dropped below flood stage at 31/2100 PST.  The Flood Warning was cancelled at 31/1656 PST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused widespread flooding over Coos and Curry counties.  Most of the flooding was minor, but some structures were inundated and mudslides closed several roads.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An Area Flood Warning was issued for Coos and Curry counties, including the Chetco River, at 28/0827 PST, effective from 28/0827 until 29/2015 PST.  The Curry Pilot reported that North Bank Chetco River Road was closed by mudslides, isolating 1000-1500 people.  Other than that, minor flooding was reported in low-lying areas.  However, the Chetco River remained in its banks.  The river exceeded action stage (18.5 feet) at 28/1115 PST, crested at 19.02 feet at 28/1215 PST, then dropped below action stage at 28/1430 PST.  The river rose above action stage again at 29/0600 PST, crested at 21.44 feet at 29/1130 PST, then dropped below action stage at 29/1645 PST.  The Flood Warning was cancelled at 29/1958 PST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved onshore on 01/08, bringing some high winds to the coastal areas of Southern Oregon.  A High Wind Warning was issued for Oregon zones ORZ021/022 at 06/2307 PST, effective from 07/2200 until 08/1000 PST.  The warning expired at 08/1000 PST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front brought plenty of wind and snow to the mountains of Southern Oregon.  A Blizzard Warning was issued for Oregon zones ORZ027/028 at 07/1558 PST, effective from 07/2200 until 08/2200 PST.  The warning was shortened until 08/1900 PST (ORZ027) and 08/1600 PST (ORZ028) at 08/1437 PST.  The warning expired at 08/1900 PST (ORZ027) and 08/1600 PST (ORZ028).  A Heavy Snow Warning was issued for Oregon zones ORZ024/025 above 1500 feet at 07/1558 PST, effective from 07/1558 until 08/0600 PST.  The warning for ORZ024 was cancelled at 08/0423 PST.  The warning for ORZ025 was extended to 08/1000 PST and raised to above 2500 feet at 08/0423 PST. The effective altitude was raised to 3000 feet at 08/0525 PST.  It was extended again to 08/1600 PST at 08/1014 PST, and yet again to 08/1900 PST at 08/1437 PST.  It finally expired at 08/1900 PST.  In addition to the west side and mountain snows, heavy snow was reported at a few east side locations as well.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An avalanche closed Highway 62 through Crater Lake National Park.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The first strong storm of the year brought winter conditions to northwest Oregon. Winds increased ahead of the approaching frontal system, and brought strong winds to the north and central Oregon coasts.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The third and final in a round of storms to end January brought winter conditions to northwest Oregon. This system brought more heavy snow to the Cascades, dramatically increasing the already high snow pack for the region. Snowfall as high as four feet fell over a 36-hour time period. Strong winds accompanied this frontal system, with high winds reported along the coast as well as gusty winds inland.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Between 25 and 48 inches inches new snowfall was recorded, based on similar conditions and reports from various stations in the north Oregon Cascades.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Warm and moist monsoonal flow created a fertile environment for thunderstorm development this day and night.  Unlike most events of this kind, the activity continued through the night.  The reports of severe hail occurred in the early morning hours of 07/22/08.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A local television station received a report from a viewer of golf ball size hail with a thunderstorm early in the day.  The exact time could not be confirmed, and the location is approximate.  No warnings were in effect.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The end of January did not bring an end to the barrage of winter storms that dumped record setting amounts of snow over Southern Oregon and Northern California.  One more storm blew in on 02/02-03/08.  In Southern Oregon, a Winter Storm Warning was issued for ORZ027/028/029/030/031 at 01/1205 PST, effective from 02/1000 until 03/1000 PST.  The warning was cancelled at 03/0201 PST.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A spotter in Wimer 7NNE of Rogue River reported 4.5 inches of snow during this interval.

Lightning from a thunderstorm event on 08/13/08 ignited a round of wildfires across southwest Oregon. The North Fork Complex fires, which consisted of the North Fork and Rattle fires, were initially considered as one entity until the North Fork fire was contained and controlled on 08/31/08.  The North Fork fire was 26 miles northeast of Glide and was around 1341 acres in size.  The cost was 7.4 million.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Lightning from a thunderstorm event on 08/16/08 ignited a round of wildfires across southwest Oregon. The Wild River Complex fires consisted of the East Fork, Whiskers, and Horse Mountain fires.  As of 09/01/08, all fires were contained.  The size of the fires was 1082 acres.  6.7 million dollars had been spent on firefighting efforts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning from a thunderstorm event on 08/16/08 ignited a round of wildfires across southwest Oregon. The Lonesome Complex fires, which consisted of the Middle Fork, Lonesome, and Bessie Rock fires.  As of 09/01/08, all but the Middle Fork fires were contained.  The size of the fires was 800 acres and the Middle Fork fire was 35% contained.  2.6 million dollars had been spent on firefighting efforts.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Monsoonal moisture moved into the Medford CWA on the 16th.  This, combined with extreme surface heating, led to the development of thunderstorms.  Some of these thunderstorms contained severe wind gusts and hail.  Temperatures cooled down drematically over this interval, but there was still enough surface heating and cooling aloft to support some thunderstorm development.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A member of the public reported that part of a barn roof was blown away, a firmly anchored car canopy was toppled, and a boat was damaged by strong gusts associated with a thunderstorm.   No warning was issued for this storm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The third in a series of an unusually cold storm systems brought heavy snow accumulations to northwest Oregon.  The heavy snowfall created a significant impact to many communities across northwest Oregon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ten to 20 inches of snow fell across the central Willamette Valley. Some reports include 10 inches in McMinnville, 17 inches in Dundee and 20 inches in Lafayette.  Freezing rain also occurred during this storm, which produced 1/4 to 1/2 inch of ice accumulation.  This snowfall combined with accumulations from the previous few days resulted in 3 roof collapses and 10 carport collapses.  The heavy snow also downed several dozen tress in the Enchanted Theme Park causing significant damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Yet another winter storm brought another round of heavy snow to Northern California and Southern Oregon. The snow level dropped to sea level during this event...bringing snow to areas that rarely get any snow.||In Southern Oregon, a Heavy Snow Warning was issued at 27/0949 PST for Oregon zones ORZ021/022/023 above 1000 feet, ORZ024 above 1500 feet, and ORZ025, effective from 27/0949 until 28/0400 PST.  ORZ026 was added to the warning area at 27/1214 PST and the warning level was lowered to the surface in ORZ024.  At 27/2200 PST, the warning was extended until 28/0800 PST for zones ORZ021/022/023 and to 28/1000 for zones ORZ024/025/026.  Also at 27/2200, the warning level was lowered to 500 feet for ORZ021/022 and to the surface for ORZ023.  All Warnings were downgraded to Snow Advisories at 28/0130 PST.  On top of all this, a Blizzard Warning was issued for Oregon zones ORZ030/031 at 27/1214 PST, effective from 27/1214 until 27/2200 PST. The warning was extended until 28/0100 PST at 27/2200 PST. The warning was cancelled at 28/0000 PST.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Based on reports of Blizzard conditions in adjacent zones ORZ029 and ORZ031, it is assumed that blizzard conditions occurred in ORZ030 as well.

A strong and cold Pacific system brought copious amounts of new snow accumulations to the higher elevations of northwest Oregon.  This system dropped snow levels considerably, leading to the first snow accumulation of the year in areas within the Columbia River Gorge and the Upper Hood River Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Between 20 and 30 inches total snowfall accumulation fell, based on similar conditions as and reports from the north Oregon Cascades.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A rapidly advancing cold front brought strong south winds to the coastline of northwest Oregon.  The strongest winds were found along the central coast.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong winds were recorded on Clatsop Spit.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another strong Pacific system brought more snow to the already snow-laden Oregon Cascades and Cascade Foothills.  This storm was particularly strong and very cold, resulting in low elevation snow as well.  Snow accumulations were reported in the Columbia River Gorge, the Upper Hood River Valley, as well as the southern Willamette Valley.  Multiple school closures occurred and were contributed to hazardous road conditions after snowfall blanketed many roads.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy freezing rain was reported from Bonneville westward through the Columbia River Gorge. Multiple injury accidents occurred due to cars sliding on the slick roadway of I-84.  One accident was fatal, killing a 71-year old woman. She was unable to stop her van, and it slid into the back of a stopped tractor-trailer, and was eventually hit a second time by a motor home. This accident, combined with multiple others, caused I-84 to be closed for a time near Corbett.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Between 18 and 28 inches accumulated snowfall fell, based on similar conditions and multiple reports from ODOT stations, SNOTEL sites, and ski resorts in the north Oregon Cascades.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The third and final in a round of storms to end January brought winter conditions to northwest Oregon. This system brought more heavy snow to the Cascades, dramatically increasing the already high snow pack for the region. Snowfall as high as four feet fell over a 36-hour time period. Strong winds accompanied this frontal system, with high winds reported along the coast as well as gusty winds inland.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High winds were recorded at Cape Foulweather, a few miles south of Depoe Bay.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moist warm front moved just south of Southeast Oregon and Southwest Idaho spreading up to fourteen inches of new snow in Baker County, eight inches in parts of Harney County and up to five inches in Malheur County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Cooperative observers at Burns and Drewsey reported seven and eight inches respectively and four inches was reported on the western edge of the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Directly after a strong Pacific storm brought heavy snow to the region, a second equally strong system dumped more snow over the northwest Oregon Cascades and Foothills. An additional two to three feet of snow blanketed the Cascades, with areas in the Foothills gaining up to a foot and a half.  Snow was also reported in the upper reaches of the Upper Hood River Valley as well as the Coast Range. This storm would be the second of three that would significantly increase the snow pack throughout the region.  Strong winds accompanied this frontal system, with high winds reported along the coast as well as gusty winds inland.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Twenty-eight inches new snowfall were reported at the Willamette Pass along Hwy. 58.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong and cold Pacific system brought copious amounts of new snow accumulations to the higher elevations of northwest Oregon.  This system dropped snow levels considerably, leading to the first snow accumulation of the year in areas within the Columbia River Gorge and the Upper Hood River Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Over the course of about 18 hours, multiple avalanches occurred in the same area, over the Santiam Pass, near the intersection of Hwy. 20 and Hwy. 126. The avalanches were contributed to higher than average snow pack, and large amounts of plowed snow along the highways. The first set of avalanches occurred the morning of January 8th, the largest burying parts of US Hwy. 20 up to 15 feet deep.  Three cars were trapped by the avalanche then later freed, with no injuries.  The second round of avalanches occurred late at night on January 8th and wasn't cleared until the following morning.  Eight commercial vehicles were trapped for a time under the snow, with no injuries reported.  Both rounds closed the highways for an extended period of time for snow removal.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong Pacific winter storm system brought high winds to the coastal region and Cascades of northwest Oregon.  The strong winds ahead of the approaching frontal system caused several power outages along the coast and downed several trees in the Cascades.

Other high wind reports include 37 knots (43 mph) with gusts to 52 knots (60 mph) at Garabaldi, and gusts to 57 knots (66 mph) in Tillamook.  Extensive power outages was also reported in Cannon Beach.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep low pressure system produced damaging winds across northeast Oregon and extreme southeast Washington. Wind damage exceeded 1 million dollars Umatilla County, especially in the Milton-Freewater and Adams areas.  There were high profile vehicles blown over, trees toppled, power outages, and roofs damaged. The screen for the drive-in theatre in Milton-Freewater was destroyed. Wind gusts in mph included LaGrande and 4 miles southeast of Imbler (61), 3 miles southeast of Enterprise (80), 3 miles east of Joseph (60), 10 miles south of Imnaha (63), Helix (73), Mission (58), John Day (71), 5 miles south southeast of Fox (68), Mitchell (66), 15 miles west of Heppner (70), Pilot Rock (58), 1 mile north northwest of Paulina (60), and Ukiah (62).

An unseasonably cold air mass settled over Southern Oregon during the last 10 days of March and first few days of April 2008.  A Freeze Warning was issued for Oregon zones ORZ024/026 at 03/31/1341 PDT, effective from 01/0300 until 01/0900 PDT. The warning expired at 01/0900 PDT.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Reported low temperatures ranged from 23 to 29 degrees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A unusually cold storm system brought heavy snow accumulations to northwest Oregon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Between 10 and 17 inches accumulated snowfall fell based on spotter reports in Vernonia and road reports on highways 6 and 26.  Some totals include 17 inches on the Wilson River Summit along highway 6; 15 inches on the Sunset Summit on highway 26 and 12 inches in Vernonia.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A unusually cold storm system brought heavy snow accumulations to northwest Oregon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Between 10 and 16 inches accumulated snowfall fell based on nearby Coast Range reports as well as road reports on highways 22 and 18.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong winter storm brought heavy snow and high winds to the area which caused blizzard conditions in the Oregon Cascades.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The third in a series of an unusually cold storm systems brought heavy snow accumulations to northwest Oregon.  The heavy snowfall created a significant impact to many communities across northwest Oregon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Between 11 and 24 inches of snow fell in the north Oregon Coast Range.  Some spotter reports include 16 inches in Buxton, 18 inches in Vernonia and 19 inches in Mist. Other reports inlcude 11 inches on the Wilson River Summit along highway 6 and 24 inches at the Seine Creek Snotel.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Between 24 and 39 inches of snow accumulation fell in the north Oregon Cascades. Some reports include 26 inches at Blue Box Pass, 35 inches at Government Camp and White River, and 39 inches at Mt Hood Meadows and Timberline.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A unusually cold storm system brought heavy snow accumulations to northwest Oregon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Between 10 and 20 inches of snow accumulation fell in the Lane County  Cascade Foothils based on similar reports from the north Oregon Cascade Foothills.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Between 18 and 30 inches of snow accumulation fell in the Lane County Cascades. Some reports include 19 inches at Santiam Pass and 24 inches at Willamette Pass.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another cold storm system brought heavy snow accumulations to northwest Oregon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Between 8 and 10 inches of snow fell in the Upper Hood River Valley.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the afternoon, in a cold and unstable airmass, thunderstorm activity formed across the forecast area. A few of these storms produced gusty winds which resulted in localized damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A strong wind gust toppled a 60 foot tree, which was approximately a foot to a foot and a half in diameter. The tree fell on a car, trapping a man inside. The man was later helped out of the car, and no injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A frontal system brought localized strong winds to the Oregon Coast.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gust reported at Clatsop Spit.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Subtropical moisture moving north from California helped to trigger numerous severe thunderstorms during the afternoons and evenings of June 29-30.
Subtropical moisture moving north from California helped to trigger numerous severe thunderstorms during the afternoons and evenings of June 29-30.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Morrow County Emergency Management reports trees down on houses with power poles and lines down in the south side of Irrigon.  Wind damage to numerous houses and buildings in the Irrigon area. At least 22 power poles down from Paterson Ferry Road to Irrigon.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Warm and moist monsoonal flow created a fertile environment for thunderstorm development this day and night.  Unlike most events of this kind, the activity continued through the night.  The reports of severe hail occurred in the early morning hours of 07/22/08.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A spotter reported 0.75 inch hail with a thunderstorm. No warnings were in effect.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the first major heat wave of the season and amid record breaking heat, two men lost their lives to injuries, and it was reported by the sheriff's department that weather may have contributed to each death. Both men were middle aged, reported to be in very good health, and were participating in some sort of physical exercise at the time of their injuries.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: During unseasonably high temperatures of 90+ degrees and after a record breaking warm day the day before, a man died during a Salem Bike Club ride. No one was with the man when he collapsed, but help was called for when he was found. Emergency workers could not revive him. The Yamhill County Sheriff's Office said it appeared he had no water with him, and heat could have contributed to his death.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the first major heat wave of the season and amid record breaking heat, two men lost their lives to injuries, and it was reported by the sheriff's department that weather may have contributed to each death. Both men were middle aged, reported to be in very good health, and were participating in some sort of physical exercise at the time of their injuries.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Record temperatures of 100+ degrees were set that day for Portland, Vancouver, and Hillsboro.  A man was running a 10K race in Sandy. Halfway through the race, police think the man veered off the course, and his body was later found in a nearby field.  He was quoted as being healthier than a horse, and police said weather likely contributed to his death.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Southerly flow brought moist and unstable air into eastern Oregon. An upper level disturbance combined with daytime heating developed numerous severe thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large hail accompanied by wind gust to 53 mph.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large hail was accompanied by heavy rainfall.

Unseasonably warm temperatures and above normal snowpack caused heavy snowmelt and was followed by heavy rainfall which led to minor flooding near Imnaha.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Imnaha River gage crested several times above flood stage with a peak of 6.26 on May 19.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Directly after a strong Pacific storm brought heavy snow to the region, a second equally strong system dumped more snow over the northwest Oregon Cascades and Foothills. An additional two to three feet of snow blanketed the Cascades, with areas in the Foothills gaining up to a foot and a half.  Snow was also reported in the upper reaches of the Upper Hood River Valley as well as the Coast Range. This storm would be the second of three that would significantly increase the snow pack throughout the region.  Strong winds accompanied this frontal system, with high winds reported along the coast as well as gusty winds inland.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High winds were recorded at Cape Foulweather.  High winds of 56 knots were also reported at Clatsop Spit.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Between 9 and 12 inches total accumulated snowfall fell, based on ODOT reports from multiple stations along Hwy. 26.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The third and final in a round of storms to end January brought winter conditions to northwest Oregon. This system brought more heavy snow to the Cascades, dramatically increasing the already high snow pack for the region. Snowfall as high as four feet fell over a 36-hour time period. Strong winds accompanied this frontal system, with high winds reported along the coast as well as gusty winds inland.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High winds were recorded on the Yaquina Bay Bridge along Hwy. 101 in Newport, with recorded sustained winds of 43 kt.  High winds of 38 knots sustained, 50 knot gusts was also reported at the Newport Jetty.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Between 15 and 22 inches inches total accumulated snowfall fell, based on reports from the Mount Hood Meadows and Timberline ski resorts on Mt. Hood, as well as ODOT reports along Hwy. 22 and Hwy. 35.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong winds were recorded at the Sea Lion Caves, about 10 miles north of Florence.  Strong winds of 50 knots were also recorded at Florence; 51 knots at Cape Foulweather and 52 knots at Yaquina Bridge in Newport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another strong Pacific system brought more snow to the already snow-laden Oregon Cascades and Cascade Foothills.  This storm was particularly strong and very cold, resulting in low elevation snow as well.  Snow accumulations were reported in the Columbia River Gorge, the Upper Hood River Valley, as well as the southern Willamette Valley.  Multiple school closures occurred and were contributed to hazardous road conditions after snowfall blanketed many roads.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Between 18 and 28 inches accumulated snowfall fell, based on multiple reports from ODOT stations, SNOTEL sites, and ski resorts.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved onshore this day...bringing gusty winds to the coast.  Only one station recorded gusts meeting High Wind Warning criteria, so it is likely that the high winds were localized as Humbug Mountain records high wind often.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The ODOT sensor at Humbug Mountain recorded wind gusts exceeding High Wind Warning criteria continuously from 21/1422 until 21/1452 PST. The peak gust was 75 mph, recorded at 21/1452 PST.  Humbug Mountain recorded another stretch of gusts meeting High Wind Warning criteria from 21/1552 until 21/1652 PST.  The peak gust was 65 mph, recorded at 21/1622 PST.

CZ_NAME: NORTHERN & EASTERN KLAMATH COUNTY & WESTERN LAKE COUNTY
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The end of January did not bring an end to the barrage of winter storms that dumped record setting amounts of snow over Southern Oregon and Northern California.  One more storm blew in on 02/02-03/08.  In Southern Oregon, a Winter Storm Warning was issued for ORZ027/028/029/030/031 at 01/1205 PST, effective from 02/1000 until 03/1000 PST. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Despite mild weather conditions, strong ocean seas and heavy surf combined to create hazardous conditions on Gleneden Beach, just north of Depoe Bay.  Two boys, ages 11 and 16, were swept out to sea by a strong rip current. One boy was rescued, but pronounced dead at a nearby hospital.  The second was never found, and the search was called off and the boy presumed dead.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An 11-year old boy was pulled out to sea by a rip current. The Coast Guard was called to rescue, an d a lifeboat and helicopter were dispatched. Lincoln City Fire and Rescue recovered the boy, and he was transported to the Lincoln City Hospital, where he was declared dead. |During the rescue of another boy, a 16-year old boy was reported missing and thought to have gone out to rescue the first boy.  The Coast Guard searched the ocean, but the boy was never found. The search was called off the next morning, and the boy was presumed dead.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: One of the first strong storms of the year brought winter conditions to northwest Oregon. Winds increased ahead of the approaching frontal system, and brought strong winds to the north and central Oregon coasts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong winds were reported at Rockaway Beach, just north of Garibaldi.  Strong winds of 53 knots was also reported at Clatsop Spit and Astoria.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Monsoonal moisture combined with strong surface heating and an upper level disturbance led to an environment conducive to thunderstorm development this day.  A number of thunderstorms did develop, but only one verified as a severe thunderstorm.  A Severe Thunderstorm Warning was issued for West Central Jackson and East Central Josephine counties at 20/1933 PDT, effective from 28/1933 PDT until 28/2015 PDT.  At 28/2016 PDT, it was extended for Northern Josephine county until 28/2045 PDT.  The warning expired at 28/2045 PDT.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A Severe Thunderstorm moved through Rogue River.  In addition to the hail, strong winds disrupted the annual Rooster Crow event in town, overturning a number of booths at the event.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Monsoonal moisture moved into the Medford CWA on the 16th.  This, combined with extreme surface heating, led to the development of thunderstorms.  Some of these thunderstorms contained severe wind gusts and hail.  Temperatures cooled down drematically over this interval, but there was still enough surface heating and cooling aloft to support some thunderstorm development.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A fire crew working a wildfire at NW Baldy Buttes 3E Howard Prairie reported nickel size hail at T38 R5 SEC8.  0.50 inch hail was reported  at 18/1620 PDT at nearby Fish Lake Resort.  A Severe Thunderstorm Warning was issued for this storm at 17/1551 PDT, effective from 17/1551 until 17/1615 PDT. The area covered was east central Jackson and southwest Klamath counties.  A new warning was issued for this storm at 17/1614 PDT, effective from 17/1614 until 17/1700 PDT. The area covered was east central Jackson and west central Klamath counties.  The warning expired at 17/1700 PDT.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: High winds knocked down many trees in the Jackson County forests, which in turn caused one death and damaged many structures and vehicles.  While the highest recorded gust in the area was 52 mph, winds must have been much higher in the Butte Falls and Lake Creek areas.  A Gold Hill woman was killed when a tree fell on her vehicle around 04/0610 PST.   Around 50 trees were reported down around Butte Falls, where the elementary school, at least two houses, and at least one vehicle were heavily damaged.  Many areas lost electricity and communications, and the power was not restored to some areas for several days.

The peak gust was 58 KT at 04/1130, 04/1145, and 04/1245 PST.  ||The Red Mound RAWS recorded verifying gusts of 53 KT at 04/0316, 56 KT at 04/0516, 62 KT at 04/0616, and 51 KT at 04/1116 PST.||The Humbug Mt. site (ODOT58) recorded verifying gusts on all but 9 obs between 04/0852 and 04/1622 PST.  The peak gust was 73 KT at 04/1237 PST.  ||Two spotter reports verified the warning.  A spotter 4NE Gold Beach recorded a gust to 52 KT at 04/0500 PST, and a spotter 1SE Brooking reported a gust to 56 KT at 04/0948 PST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Multiple storm systems contributed to a historic heavy snow event over Northern California from 01/03 to 01/05/08.  While the brunt of the storm hit central and northern California, some severe winter weather occurred in Southwest Oregon as well.  The event started off as a wind and snow event with isolated blizzard conditions.  The winds diminished on 01/04, but the heavy snow continued and lowered in altitude through the evening of 01/05.  Many Snow Advisories were issued for this storm, but no warnings until the snow level dropped on 01/05.  That event will be covered separately.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A spotter 4WSW of Howard Prairie Lake at Hyatt Lake reported whiteout/blizzard conditions during this interval with 3-4 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A localized Heavy Snow Event occurred on January 5-6, with the Applegate and Illinois Valleys bearing the brunt of the snow.  A Heavy Snow Warning (upgraded from a Snow Advisory) was issued for Oregon zone ORZ024 above 1500 feet at 05/1012 PST, effective from 05/1012 until 06/0600 PST.  It was downgraded to a Snow Advisory at 05/2112 PST.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A spotter 3N Merlin at 1550 feet reported 7 inches of snow in 24 hours ending at 07/0700 PST.  A spotter 2NNE Selma reported 8 inches of snow in 24 hours ending at 07/0800 PST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Narrative.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A lightning strike damaged electrical and computer equipment at a sewage treatment plant, causing a back-up in sewage treatment and discharge, as well as extensive damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Lightning from a thunderstorm event on 08/16/08 ignited a round of wildfires across southwest Oregon. The Lonesome Complex fires consisted of the Middle Fork, Lonesome, and Bessie Rock fires.  As of 09/01/08, all but the Middle Fork fires were contained.  The size of the fires was 800 acres and the Middle Fork fire was 35% contained.  2.6 million dollars had been spent on firefighting efforts.

The size of the fires was 800 acres and the Middle Fork fire was 35% contained.  2.6 million dollars had been spent on firefighting efforts.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the evening after a particularly active couple of days with showers and thunderstorms, one storm strengthened to the severe category, and produced rather large hail for this particular area of the country.

Under an unstable air mass, showers and scattered thunderstorms moved over the forecast area. A few of these thunderstorms became severe enough to produce dime to penny sized hail along with gusty winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A spotter called in and reported dime-sized hail, with some slightly larger hailstones mixed in.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Gnarl Ridge Fire was started by lightning on Thursday night, August 7, 2008 on the north flank of Mt. Hood. It was contained by late August at 516 acres.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The fire was located on Mt. Hood, and reinitialized later in September.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Multiple lightning storms started over sixty fires in an approximately 500,000 acre area in the south zone of the Willamette National Forest. Fifty-two of the fires were confirmed, and over 200 acres in total were burned.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The complex was located near Oakridge, Oregon.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall, fueled by a tropical moisture source that had origins in the west Pacific, drenched the region over the course of a few days. This heavy rainfall caused flooding along multiple rivers in northwest Oregon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding due to heavy rain in the Coast Range caused multiple closures along Hwy. 101 north of and inside downtown Tillamook.  The flooding also affected local businesses along the highway, where flood waters in some places were near two feet high. NOTE: Damage estimate is for the entire Tillamook county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall, fueled by a tropical moisture source that had origins in the west Pacific, drenched the region over the course of a few days. This heavy rainfall caused flooding along multiple rivers in northwest Oregon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Wilson River Hwy., or OR Hwy. 6, was closed for a time period due to a mudslide that covered the highway, about 15 miles east of Tillamook.  Heavy rains in the Coast Range which contributed to the flooding of the Wilson River also triggered the mudslide.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A sneaker wave swept two people, a boy and his uncle, into the ocean near Simpson Beach, south of Coos Bay.  The people were standing on rocks near the water when a knee high wave broke into them.  The six year old boy was pulled from the water, but died en route to the hospital.  As of 10/29/08, the 39 year old uncle was still missing.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A boy and his uncle were swept into the water by a rogue wave.  No warning or advisories were in effect at the time.  Buoy 229 offshore reported west to northwest swell with heights of 3.2 to 3.7 meters and a period of 15 to 17 seconds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On September 16, unusually hot and dry conditions coupled with a persistent thermal belt during the night caused the persistent Gnarl Ridge Fire to make a major run shortly after midnight.  Fire lines were established around the fire, much of the heat was extinguished, some of the hazardous trees were felled, and drainage was established along roads and fire lines. The historic Cloud Cap Inn and the structures at Tilly Jane were saved from the fire.  The fire was not contained until the first few days of October.  In the aftermath, over 3000 acres were affected.

Lightning from a thunderstorm event on 08/17/08 ignited another round of wildfires across southwest Oregon. The Rattle Fire was initially part of the North Fork Complex, but the other fires were suppressed. As of 09/30/08, the size of the fire was 18838 acres and was 65% contained. No updated expenditures was available, but as of 08/31/08, 1.9 million dollars had been spent on firefighting efforts.

No updated expenditures was available, but as of 08/31/08, 1.9 million dollars had been spent on firefighting efforts.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Unseasonably warm temperatures and above normal snowpack caused heavy snowmelt and was followed by heavy rainfall which led to flooding in portions of Union County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding along Catherine Creek and the Grande Ronde River damaged several roads in Union County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall, fueled by a tropical moisture source that had origins in the west Pacific, drenched the region over the course of a few days. This heavy rainfall caused flooding along multiple rivers in northwest Oregon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain draining into local rivers, including the Trask River and nearby Tillamook River, caused local flooding.  Flooding along Fawcett Creek, which drains into the Tillamook, washed out a bridge along S. Prairie Road. Two vehicles containing 8 people total had to be rescued from the rushing water after trying to cross the washed out bridge. No major injuries were reported.
